lens.included_activities: Independent US lower-middle-market contract and private-label manufacturers repeatedly formulating, producing, and packaging nonchocolate confections such as gummies, hard candy, mints, chews, caramels, lozenges, and taffy for external brands and retailers.
lens.excluded_activities: Manufacturers primarily selling their own brands, retail candy shops making on premises, supplement-only plants whose primary classification is outside 311340, captive facilities, pure co-packers without confectionery manufacturing, and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Recurring product-development, batch-conversion, and packaging revenue from brands and retailers, generally priced per unit, case, or production run under purchase orders, fixed-term supply agreements, or periodically rebid private-label programs.
lens.deviation_from_default: The NAICS code includes branded manufacturers, private-label producers, contract manufacturers, and on-premises makers. It is narrowed to contract and private-label manufacturing because only that subset supplies a recurring outsourced service to external customers.

a.bridge: BLS reports the broader sugar and confectionery sector, not 311340 contract manufacturers: production is 42.74% of employment, transportation/material moving 15.26%, maintenance 5.54%, office support 5.73%, management 5.40%, and sales 13.95%. I map most scheduling, customer communication, formulation search, artwork/specification review, demand planning, QA documentation, inspection analysis, and back-office tasks to AI exposure, plus selected machine-monitoring work. I treat cooking, depositing, curing, finishing, sanitation, changeovers, maintenance, and material handling as mostly physical. The broader category's retail sales share likely biases exposure upward for industrial co-manufacturers, while higher-paid technical and management work makes wage-weighted exposure higher than a raw headcount mapping.
a.rationale: Co-manufacturers combine physical candy production with customer-specific formulation, quoting, specification, compliance, scheduling, and quality workflows. AI has substantial reach in the information layer but only selective reach into hands-on line work without additional robotics and sensors.
a.change_evidence: Payroll-weighted occupation rosters and task-level time studies from gummy, hard-candy, mint, and chew co-manufacturers, together with current automation inventories, would materially tighten a.
caveats: The occupation source is four-digit NAICS and includes chocolate and sugar manufacturing.
Small-batch and artisan operations may have substantially more manual work than scaled gummy or hard-candy lines.
Existing recipe, depositing, wrapping, inspection, and warehouse automation is unknown by firm.

rho.bridge: US manufacturing surveys report 51% current AI use and 60% expected use by 2027, while Federal Reserve synthesis shows adoption varying greatly by firm size, industry, and survey definition. Neither measures stable labor-saving implementation in LMM confectionery co-manufacturers. I discount for fragmented systems, limited technical staff, food-safety validation, customer approvals, seasonal peaks, and changeovers. Manufacturing-wide respondents likely bias implementation upward; accessible office tools and vision systems partially offset that bias.
rho.rationale: Quoting, customer service, documentation, demand planning, and production scheduling can adopt quickly. Vision inspection, predictive maintenance, process optimization, and automated QA are feasible but require reliable plant data and controlled change, so only part of exposed work should be implemented within five years.
rho.change_evidence: Implementation audits from 10 to 15 LMM confectionery co-manufacturers showing production AI use cases, labor hours avoided, scrap, uptime, false rejects, audit findings, customer approvals, and time to stable use would materially change rho.
caveats: Survey use may include pilots or individual productivity tools with no labor substitution.
Gummy, hard-candy, panning, and lozenge processes have different technical constraints.
Food and supplement gummies may face different quality and regulatory systems.

e.rationale: Contract and private-label manufacturing is visibly established: multiple US firms advertise flexible candy production, a trade sourcebook reports private-label hard-candy capability among 44% of its participating manufacturers, and a current directory lists 73 private-label candy manufacturers across chocolate and nonchocolate categories. Against this, 2018 retail data put private label at only 6.4% of nonchocolate sales, and many NAICS firms are branded or on-premises makers. Applying these mixed signals to 120 dataset-estimated LMM firms supports a meaningful minority, not a majority, as eligible.
e.change_evidence: A verified census of the 120 estimated LMM firms showing NAICS primary activity, EBITDA, external contract/private-label revenue share, ownership, and whether manufacturing rather than packaging is performed would resolve e.
caveats: Supplier directories are promotional, may duplicate facilities, and include chocolate or nutraceutical firms outside 311340.
Retail private-label share understates branded products made by third-party contract manufacturers.
The frozen n estimate is margin-bridged and does not enumerate actual firms.

s5.bridge: Gallup measures five-year ownership intentions among US employer-business owners, EPI measures broad owner readiness, and BizBuySell tracks reported Main Street transactions across sectors. I translate broad succession pressure and stronger 2024 manufacturing transaction growth into a completed qualifying-control-transfer probability, discounting for failed sales, closures, internal transfers, and firms that are not transferable without founder recipes or customer relationships. Broad surveys may overstate completed third-party deals, while fragmented family ownership may make this niche more active than large food manufacturing.
s5.rationale: Many independent confectionery manufacturers are long-lived private or family businesses, and the broader manufacturing acquisition market is active. Specialized equipment, food-safety history, customer concentration, and founder dependence can prevent intended exits from closing.
s5.change_evidence: PitchBook, GF Data, Axial, Candy Industry, and broker records for US gummy, hard-candy, mint, chew, and private-label confectionery control deals from 2016 through 2026, divided by a verified eligible-firm denominator, would materially move s5.
caveats: No source directly measures qualifying transfers for the lens.
Owner intentions do not equal closed control transactions.
Supplement-gummy deals may be classified outside 311340.

q.bridge: A filed food contract-manufacturing agreement itemizes materials, packaging, conversion fees, and ingredient-cost reconciliation, while confectionery evidence shows significant retailer and distributor concentration and periodic private-label competition. These are not observed LMM nonchocolate-candy contracts. I infer that savings can be retained during fixed terms but are partly transferred through renewal negotiations, open-book costing, competitive bids, or customer demands. Large public-company customer concentration may overstate buyer power for differentiated small-batch co-manufacturers; transparent per-unit bids may understate retention.
q.rationale: Customization, formulation know-how, audit history, tooling, minimum runs, and reliable seasonal capacity create switching costs. Yet brand and retailer customers can rebid programs, benchmark per-unit conversion, own formulas, and demand productivity concessions, limiting five-year retention.
q.change_evidence: Anonymized MSAs and bid histories from at least 10 eligible firms showing term, formula ownership, cost transparency, indexation, productivity clauses, tooling ownership, renewal price changes, and customer concentration would materially change q.
caveats: The contract evidence is from adjacent food manufacturing rather than candy.
Seasonal capacity scarcity and customer power vary sharply by product and run size.
Demand-volume effects are excluded and captured in d5 and o.

d5.bridge: NCA reports nonchocolate confectionery dollar sales grew by almost 70% from 2019 to 2024 and AP reports 4.9% dollar growth in 2024, while Circana shows nonchocolate gaining Halloween volume share versus chocolate. These are retail category measures, not constant-price outsourced-manufacturing quantity. I strip out much of the nominal growth and allow a modest five-year quantity gain from gummies, innovation, private label, and relative affordability. Inflation makes dollar growth optimistic; outsourcing share gains could make lens demand stronger than category units.
d5.rationale: Nonchocolate candy benefits from gummies, novelty, seasonal use, functional formats, and lower exposure to cocoa costs. Health concerns, sugar reduction, GLP-1 adoption, imports, and fads create downside, but repeated brand innovation and retailer assortment support modest real quantity growth.
d5.change_evidence: Circana or NielsenIQ unit volumes by gummies, hard candy, mints, chews, private label, functional candy, and channel, plus eligible firms' contracted volumes, customer pipelines, and churn, would materially tighten d5.
caveats: Published growth is primarily dollar based and includes price and mix.
Halloween share shifts may reflect cocoa inflation rather than durable preference.
The current outsourced-service basket may grow faster or slower than branded retail sales.

o.bridge: FDA rules require covered candy facilities to register, apply CGMPs, analyze hazards, maintain preventive controls, and document food-safety systems; an FDA candy-facility warning letter illustrates that physical sanitation and equipment conditions remain operator responsibilities. These sources establish accountable physical production but do not measure the share of year-five contract demand retained by current lens operators. They may bias o upward because brands can insource or shift to larger or offshore co-manufacturers.
o.rationale: Software cannot physically cook, deposit, cure, coat, form, wrap, clean, release, and ship candy. Brands without plants will still require accountable manufacturers, though some volume may be insourced, imported, or consolidated into large automated providers.
o.change_evidence: Five-year make-versus-buy plans from confectionery brands and retailers, co-manufacturer contract renewal and churn data, announced domestic and offshore capacity, and evidence of autonomous line operation would materially move o.
caveats: Regulation ensures an accountable facility, not survival of a particular operator.
Supplement gummies may shift to differently regulated or classified plants.
Automation can reduce staffing without eliminating the operator entity.

narrative.executive_view: Nonchocolate confectionery contains a visible contract and private-label manufacturing niche with recurring customer production, a sizable estimated firm base, and category demand supported by gummies and innovation. AI opportunity is concentrated in planning, quality, customer, and process-information workflows rather than the core physical craft.
narrative.ai_impact: AI can accelerate quoting, formulation search, demand forecasting, scheduling, specification and artwork review, QA documentation, customer service, machine vision, predictive maintenance, and root-cause analysis. Cooking, depositing, curing, coating, sanitation, changeovers, packaging interventions, and maintenance remain substantially physical.
narrative.value_capture: Formulation know-how, validated facilities, tooling, audit history, and scarce seasonal capacity support retention. Per-unit bidding, customer-owned recipes, cost transparency, concentrated buyers, and renewal competition should return a meaningful share of gains to brands and retailers over five years.
narrative.firm_availability: Supplier directories and operating company sites confirm many contract and private-label offerings, but they also mix chocolate, supplements, packaging, and foreign capacity. A firm-by-firm screen is necessary before applying the 120-firm frozen estimate to the target lens.
narrative.demand_durability: Nonchocolate candy has recently outgrown chocolate in dollar terms and gained seasonal volume share, aided by gummies, novelty, and relative affordability. Constant-price quantity growth should be more modest, while physical outsourced production remains strongly operator-required.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: Key risks are uncertain eligible share, customer concentration, private-label rebids, food-safety and allergen failures, seasonal working capital, formula and trend obsolescence, sugar and ingredient volatility, imported competition, and overestimating labor savings in manual small-batch plants.
narrative.principal_driver: A real and recurring contract-manufacturing niche benefits from gummy-led demand, innovation cadence, and information-heavy customer and quality workflows.
narrative.principal_weakness: Eligibility and contract economics are poorly enumerated, and much factory labor remains physical while sophisticated customers can reclaim savings at renewal.

sources.title: 2022 NAICS definition: Nonchocolate Confectionery Manufacturing
sources.supports: Identifies 311340 as nonchocolate confectionery manufacturing and establishes the product-defined industry boundary.

sources.title: May 2023 OEWS: Sugar and Confectionery Product Manufacturing
sources.supports: Reports broader-industry employment and wages, including 42.74% production, 15.26% transportation/material moving, 5.54% maintenance, 5.73% office support, 5.40% management, and 13.95% sales.

sources.title: Sprinkk Confectionery Manufacturing
sources.supports: Shows a flexible contract-manufacturing facility producing gummies, fruit snacks, hard candy, chews, caramels, lozenges, mints, and taffy.

sources.title: Primrose Candy Company
sources.supports: Advertises contract manufacturing, packaging, private label, four hard-candy cooking systems, and 16 manufacturing lines.

sources.title: NAM to White House: Here's How to Boost AI Adoption in Manufacturing
sources.supports: Reports a 2025 survey in which 51% of manufacturers used AI, 60% expected to by 2027, and 80% said AI would be vital by 2030.

sources.title: Monitoring AI Adoption in the US Economy
sources.supports: Reports firm and worker AI adoption measures, manufacturing adoption growth, and substantial differences caused by sampling, firm size, industry, and survey definitions.

sources.title: Confectionery Contract and Private Label Manufacturing Source Book
sources.supports: Reports private-label hard-candy production capability from 44% of sourcebook manufacturers and candy-cane capability from 27%, confirming a developed contract/private-label supplier segment.

sources.title: Candy Manufacturers Private Label - US Supplier Directory
sources.supports: Lists 73 US private-label candy manufacturers as of January 2026, while its entries span artisan chocolate, gummies, and other confectionery formats.

sources.title: Most Small-Business Owners Lack a Succession Plan
sources.supports: Reports a fall 2024 survey asking owners about the next five years and finds broad sell-or-transfer intentions among employer-business owners, with limited succession preparation.

sources.title: State of Owner Readiness
sources.supports: Reports that 51% of the current American business market is owned by Baby Boomers expected to transition over zero to ten years.

sources.title: BizBuySell Insight Report - 2024 Market Trends
sources.supports: Reports 9,546 closed small-business transactions in 2024, up 5%, with manufacturing among the sectors showing the strongest acquisition growth.

sources.title: FSMA Final Rule for Preventive Controls for Human Food
sources.supports: Requires covered food facilities to apply CGMPs, analyze hazards, maintain preventive controls, and operate a food-safety plan with verification activities as appropriate.

sources.title: Contract Manufacturing and Packaging Agreement
sources.supports: Shows an adjacent food-manufacturing contract separating material, packaging, conversion, and waste-factor costs, reconciling ingredient variances, and fixing conversion cost for the initial term.

sources.title: Tootsie Roll Industries 2025 Form 10-K
sources.supports: Identifies major retailer and distributor customers and states that losing significant customers could materially affect a confectionery manufacturer's business.

sources.title: US non-chocolate confectionery market share by company, 2018
sources.supports: Reports 2018 US retail shares of 18.4% Mars, 16.1% Ferrara, 11.5% Hershey, 6.7% Mondelez, 6.4% private label, and 40.9% all other.

sources.title: Confectionery Sales Break Records, Surpass $54 Billion in 2024
sources.supports: Reports US confectionery sales above $54 billion in 2024 and nonchocolate candy sales up nearly $5 billion, almost 70%, since 2019.

sources.title: Why trick-or-treaters may bag more gummy candy than chocolate
sources.supports: Reports 4.9% growth in US nonchocolate candy dollar sales in 2024 and a shift in Halloween volume share toward nonchocolate candy in 2025.

sources.title: Circana 2025 Halloween Candy Trends
sources.supports: Provides current consumer and seasonal context comparing chocolate and nonchocolate Halloween purchasing under elevated cocoa prices.

sources.title: FDA Warning Letter to New England Confectionery Company
sources.supports: Documents candy-facility preventive-control and sanitation responsibilities, including observed residue on extruders used for Necco Wafers and Sweethearts.
